10.1016/j.jmp.2017.11.001
In knowledge space theory (KST;  Doignon and Falmagne, 1985, 1999 ;  Falmagne and Doignon, 2011 ) the knowledge state of a student is the set of all problems that she/he masters about a specific knowledge domain. A knowledge structure is the collection of all possible knowledge states in a given population. By definition, such collection has to include at least the empty set and the total set of items. The basic local independence model (BLIM;  Doignon and Falmagne, 1999 ;  Falmagne and Doignon, 1988a ) is a probabilistic model, widely applied in KST, for the stochastic assessment of knowledge, and for the empirical validation of knowledge structures  (Anselmi, Robusto, Stefanutti, & de Chiusole, 2016; Bottesi, Spoto, Freeston, Sanavio, & Vidotto, 2015; de Chiusole & Stefanutti, 2013; de Chiusole, Stefanutti, Anselmi, & Robusto, 2013, 2015a, b; de Chiusole, Stefanutti, & Spoto, 2017; Doignon, Falmagne, & Cosyn, 2013; Falmagne, Albert, Doble, Eppstein, & Hu, 2013; Heller & Wickelmaier, 2013; Schrepp, 2005; Spoto, Stefanutti, & Vidotto, 2010; Stefanutti & Robusto, 2009; Ünlü, 2011) . It is a particular case of a Latent Class Model  (Goodman, 1974; Lazarsfeld & Henry, 1968; McHugh, 1956)  which introduces specific restrictions deserving special attention. Some connections between the BLIM and latent class analysis have been pointed out by  Ünlü (2006) . This model has lately received some attention, particularly concerning its identifiability  (Heller, 2017; Spoto, Stefanutti, & Vidotto, 2012, 2013; Ünlü, 2007, 2011) .

More specifically,  Spoto et al. (2012, 2013)  have shown that the BLIM is not identifiable for two broad classes of knowledge structures named, respectively, forward-graded and backward-graded. In those articles the analysis was confined to gradedness of the structure in a single item only. In particular, it was shown that, under gradedness in an item  
                         q 
                      , there exists more than one set of parameter values of the BLIM predicting the same probability distribution on the observed response patterns and, for this reason, the BLIM is not identifiable. An important aspect about graded structures, emerging from this recent literature, is that many commonly used structures happen to be forward- and/or backward-graded. In fact, it has been shown that structures like quasi-ordinal spaces and learning spaces are characterized by this type of gradation. Such structures are widely and fruitfully applied in practice, providing one of the reasons for the investigations carried out in this article, where further theoretical results on the unidentifiability of the BLIM with graded knowledge structures are provided. The aim is to cover the general situation in which a knowledge structure is graded in a set of items, and to describe the type of unidentifiability that occurs in this situation as a property of certain kinds of continuous transformations acting on the BLIM’s parameter space. The set of such transformations forms a commutative group under the function composition operator and it is shown that the values of the prediction function (points in the outcome space) of the BLIM are invariant with respect to anyone transformation in the group.

One of the possible benefits of such transformational approach to unidentifiability is that it helps understanding and describing the specific relations (trade-offs) existing among the unidentifiable parameters of a model.

It is shown that this transformational method can be used to test identifiability at both local and global levels. In connection with this, we shed light on the relation between this approach to the study of identifiability of a model, and the standard methods based on the study of the Jacobian matrix.

The article is organized as follows: Section  2  summarizes the fundamental concepts of KST and the BLIM; Section  3  is a brief recap of the existing theoretical results about the identifiability of the BLIM with a special attention to forward- and backward-graded knowledge structures; Section  4  introduces the basic parameter transformation groups that arise with knowledge structures that are graded in a single item; Section  5  generalizes the theoretical results to the case of a knowledge structure that is graded in a set of items; some examples to show how the theoretical results obtained in the former part of the paper apply in practice are presented in Section  7 ; in Section  8  the new theoretical results are discussed. Some final remarks discuss how these theoretical results can be used to go beyond forward- and backward-graded structures identifiability analysis.

In KST a  knowledge domain  is the set  
                         Q 
                       of all problems (items) that can be formulated in a given topic (throughout the paper  
                         Q 
                       is assumed to be finite). Given the domain  
                         Q 
                      , a  knowledge state  is the set  
                         K 
                         ⊆ 
                         Q 
                       that an individual is able to solve, and a  knowledge structure  is a pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         K 
                       represents a collection of knowledge states, containing at least  
                         ∅ 
                       and  
                         Q 
                      .

A knowledge state is, by definition, a latent object whose observable counterpart is the so called  response pattern  
                      
                         R 
                         ⊆ 
                         Q 
                       representing the set of items that an individual answered correctly. Since the observed response may be noisy due to the possibility of lucky guesses and careless errors, the response pattern of an individual could differ from his/her knowledge state. In KST the link between states and response patterns is modeled in a probabilistic fashion. A  probabilistic knowledge structure  (PKS;  Doignon and Falmagne, 1999 ) is a triple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            , 
                            π 
                            ) 
                         
                       where

                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a knowledge structure and  
                         π 
                       is a probability distribution on  
                         K 
                      . The probability of an arbitrary response pattern  
                         R 
                         ⊆ 
                         Q 
                       is defined  (Falmagne & Doignon, 1988b)  by the following unrestricted latent class model:

                                  R

                         K

                               K 
                            
                         
                       is the probability of  
                         K 
                      .

The probabilistic model most frequently applied in KST is the BLIM  (Doignon & Falmagne, 1999; Falmagne & Doignon, 1988a) , in which the answers to the items are locally independent given the knowledge state of the person. The conditional probability of a pattern given a state is determined by two error parameters of each item

This assumption appears to be adequate since it asserts that the probability of observing a wrong response to an item is higher when the student does not master the item than when the student does master it. On the other hand, the probability of observing a correct answer is assumed to be higher when the student actually masters the item rather than when she does not master it (i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         1 
                         − 
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      ). By this assumption it is expected that the probability of a correct answer to an item  
                         q 
                       monotonically increases with the probability that  
                         q 
                       belongs to the knowledge state. This type of monotonicity is only respected if  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q

                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         1 
                      . This assumption stays true throughout the present article.

Having introduced the necessary deterministic and probabilistic concepts of KST, it is now possible to focus on the notion of identifiability of a probabilistic model with particular reference to the BLIM case.

Whenever a probabilistic model is empirically tested, its identifiability is a crucial issue that must be addressed. A probabilistic model can be regarded as a triple  
                         
                            ( 
                            Θ 
                            , 
                            Ω 
                            , 
                            ϕ 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         Θ 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               m 
                            
                         
                       is the parameter space of the model,  
                         Ω 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       is the outcome space, and  
                         ϕ 
                         : 
                         Θ 
                         → 
                         Ω 
                       is a mapping called the  prediction function , assigning to each parameter vector  
                         θ 
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                       a corresponding element  
                         ω 
                         ∈ 
                         Ω 
                       in the outcome space. As far as probabilistic models like the BLIM are concerned, a point in the outcome space  
                         Ω 
                       is a probability mass distribution. In the BLIM, it is a distribution on the collection of the response patterns.

A model is said to be (globally) identifiable whenever  
                         ϕ 
                       is injective. If a model is not globally identifiable, it is still possible to test whether it is locally identifiable. A model is said to be  locally identifiable  at a point  
                         θ 
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                       if the prediction function  
                         ϕ 
                       is injective when restricted to points with distance less than some  
                         ϵ 
                         > 
                         0 
                       from  
                         θ 
                        (Bamber & Van Santen, 2000) .

The relationship between global and local identifiability has the form of an implication from the former to the latter: every globally identifiable model is also locally identifiable at every point  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                       of the parameter space. The converse implication, however, does not hold in general. What is particularly important here is that a model that is not globally identifiable could still exhibit local identifiability at certain points of the parameter space. In many relevant cases, this is a sufficient condition for making consistent statistical inference on the model parameters (see, e.g.,  McHugh, 1956 ).

In the special case of the BLIM, a point in the parameter space is a vector  
                         θ 
                       containing the  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       values for each item and a probability  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         
                            ( 
                            0 
                            , 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       for each knowledge state, with the two restrictions: (1)  
                         
                            
                               ∑ 
                            
                            
                               K 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K

                         1 
                      , and (2)  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         + 
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         < 
                         1 
                       for all  
                         q 
                         ∈ 
                         Q 
                      . The BLIM is a particular case of Latent Class Models (LCM;  Lazarsfeld & Henry, 1968 ). Nevertheless, it introduces some specific issues that deserve some further attention: (i) it has been shown by  Spoto et al. (2012, 2013)  that its identifiability strongly depends on the specific knowledge structure to which it is applied. This issue is specific for the BLIM, and it is not shared by LCMs in general; (ii) it introduces the restriction of local independence; (iii) it establishes a link between the conditional probabilities of the response patterns and the error rates, as described by Eq.  (1) .

Let  
                         ϕ 
                       denote the prediction function of the BLIM for a fixed, finite knowledge structure  
                         
                            ( 
                            Q 
                            , 
                            K 
                            ) 
                         
                      . Given a parameter vector  
                         θ 
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                      ,  
                         ϕ 
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a probability distribution on the response patterns  
                         R 
                         ⊆ 
                         Q 
                      . For a single response pattern  
                         R 
                      , the prediction function is  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ϕ 
                                  
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  θ 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ∑ 
                                  
                                  
                                     K 
                                     ∈ 
                                     K 
                                  
                               
                               P

                                  R

                                     K 
                                  
                               
                               .

Concerning BLIM identifiability, some recent theoretical results can be found in  Spoto et al. (2012, 2013)  and they are briefly summarized here. Moreover,  Stefanutti, Heller, Anselmi, and Robusto (2012)  developed a procedure for testing the local identifiability of the BLIM with arbitrary knowledge structures on sets of items having a moderate size (up to about 20–25 items). The procedure is based on the computation of the rank of the Jacobian matrix of the BLIM’s prediction function, calculated at a given point  
                         θ 
                       of the parameter space. The procedure does not provide any analytical tool for drawing conclusions about the identifiability of the BLIM by looking at the combinatorial properties of the knowledge structure. Furthermore its results are valid locally, at a given point in the parameter space.

The approach followed here, as well as in  Heller (2017)  and  Spoto et al. (2012, 2013)  is different in the specific sense that the aim is to establish which combinatorial properties of the knowledge structure make the BLIM unidentifiable, without reference to any particular point in the parameter space.

The notions of forward-graded and backward-graded knowledge structures play a fundamental role with respect to the BLIM’s identifiability, and elsewhere in this article. They are defined as follows: A knowledge structure

                       is said to be  backward-graded  (BG) in an item  
                         q

                            }

                         K

                         q

                         K

                         K

Each of the equations in  (2)  is called a  trade-off equation  for a BG knowledge structure, because it gives rise to a functional relation between some of the parameters of the model, such that any change in one parameter corresponds to a change in other parameters, but the value of the prediction function remains the same. If the knowledge structure is BG in  
                         q 
                       then, fixing

                      . These results show that if the knowledge structure is BG or FG in one item then the BLIM is not identifiable.

Drawing upon previous work on the identifiability of the BLIM  (Spoto et al., 2013) , two fundamental types of continuous parameter transformations are examined that, with BG or FG knowledge structures, keep constant the distribution on the set of response patterns (i.e., the value of the prediction function of the BLIM) and, thus, make the model unidentifiable.

Functions of this type map a point in the parameter space to another point, while keeping constant the probability distribution on the response patterns. In the sequel two special types of outcome preserving transformations are defined and examined. They are named the  backward  and the  forward  parameter transformations.

When the parameter  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  R 
                                of the function  
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                is set fixed, and  
                                  θ 
                                is free to vary, a function mapping  
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                into itself is obtained which is named  backward parameter transformation , and it is denoted  
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                  
                                (so that, for  
                                  θ 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Θ 
                               ,  
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     )

                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               ).

It should be observed that the function  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  x 
                               
                            
                          is defined over the whole  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                          and, hence its domain and codomain may contain points that lie outside the parameter space. Later on, conditions will be examined under which  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  x 
                               
                            
                          actually maps  
                            Θ 
                          into itself. Having  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  x 
                               
                            
                          that maps  
                            Θ 
                          into itself is a sufficient condition for keeping constant the probability distribution on the response patterns, thusexhibiting the outcome preserving property and showing that backward gradedness is a specific source of unidentifiability of the BLIM.

Example 1 
                            For  
                                  Q 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     c 
                                     } 
                                  
                                and the knowledge structure  
                                  K 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     { 
                                     ∅ 
                                     , 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        a 
                                        , 
                                        b 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        c 
                                        } 
                                     
                                     , 
                                     Q 
                                     } 
                                  
                               , consider the parameter vector  
                                  θ 
                                  = 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     β 
                                     , 
                                     η

                            With respect to this example, three observations are in order: first, in moving from  
                                  θ 
                                to  
                                  
                                     
                                        θ

                               , is referred to as a  trade-off  (see, e.g.,  Stefanutti et al., 2012 ;  Heller, 2017 ). Second, the new set of probabilities assigned to the four knowledge states by the backward transformation  
                                  
                                     
                                        b 
                                     
                                     
                                        c 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     , 
                                     θ 
                                     )

                                        θ

                                  θ 
                                and  
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        ′ 
                                     
                                  
                                are equivalent in the sense that they map to the same point in the outcome space. Hence, for the knowledge structure at hand the BLIM is not identifiable.

                                        b

Backward transformations for a given item  
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                          form an abelian group, isomorphic to the real additive group. This provides a geometric way of looking at trade-offs caused by backward gradedness in an item. An introduction to group theory can be found, for instance, in  Aschbacher (2000) ,  Borel (1991) ,  Lyndon and Schupp (2001) , and  Rotman (2012) .  Appendix  provides the essential concepts used in this article.

Forward transformations are the second type of continuous parameter transformations considered in this article. They are in a duality relation with the backward transformations in a precise sense. Because of this duality, most of the theoretical results that are obtainable for the backward transformations, also hold for the forward ones. Their definition is as follows.

Definition 3 
                            For every item  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q

When the parameter  
                                  x 
                                  ∈ 
                                  R 
                                of the transformation is fixed, and  
                                  θ 
                                is free to vary, a function mapping  
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                into itself is obtained which is named  forward parameter transformation , and it is denoted  
                                  
                                     
                                        f

                                  θ

                                  Θ

                                     θ

                                        q

                               ).

It has been observed in Section  3  that a knowledge structure  
                            K 
                          is FG in an item  
                            q 
                            ∈ 
                            Q 
                          if and only if its dual is BG in  
                            q 
                         . This duality between FG and BG knowledge structures induces a corresponding “duality” between forward and backward transformations.

By virtue of this duality, all results obtained so far for the backward transformations also hold, in the dual knowledge structure, for the forward transformations. For the sake of completeness, they are summarized in the following statement.

                               , if there is  
                               
                                  x

                            q 
                            ∈

                         . It is worth observing that, in the special case

                            q

                               ( 
                               B 
                               ∪ 
                               F 
                               )

                               }

Let  
                            
                               
                                  T 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            = 
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            ∪ 
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                          denote the union of these two groups. It is the set of all the forward and backward transformations generated by  
                            q 
                         . It should be observed that this set fails to be a group since it is not closed under function composition (e.g.,  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                               
                                  x 
                               
                            
                            ∘

                                  y 
                               
                            
                         , for  
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ∈ 
                            R 
                          is not in the set).

For how they have been defined, backward and forward transformations turn out to be actions of the real additive group on the whole real space  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                         . However, for rather obvious reasons, the only interesting transformations are those that move a point  
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                            ⊂ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                          to a different one, which is still in the parameter space  
                            Θ 
                         . In this section we examine conditions characterizing transformations of this type.

Definition 4 
                            For  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               , a transformation  
                                  t 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        B 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∪ 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                is  effective  at a point  
                                  θ 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Θ 
                                of the parameter space if  
                                  t 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ≠ 
                                  θ 
                               . It is  inner  at the point  
                                  θ 
                                if  
                                  t 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  Θ 
                               .

For  
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Q 
                               , a transformation  
                                  t 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        T 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                  = 
                                  
                                     
                                        B 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                  ∪ 
                                  
                                     
                                        F 
                                     
                                     
                                        q 
                                     
                                  
                                is  effective  at a point  
                                  θ 
                                  ∈ 
                                  Θ 
                                of the parameter space if  
                                  t 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ≠ 
                                  θ 
                               . It is  inner  at the point  
                                  θ 
                                if  
                                  t 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     θ 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∈ 
                                  Θ 
                               .

Thus, the only interesting transformations are those that turn out to be both effective and inner at some point  
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         . Concerning effectiveness, when the initial point  
                            θ 
                          is set fixed, the backward and forward transformations  
                            
                               
                                  b 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               x 
                               , 
                               θ 
                               ) 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  r 
                               
                            
                            
                               ( 
                               y 
                               , 
                               θ 
                               ) 
                            
                          are one-to-one.

Theorem 5 
                            
                               Given  
                               
                                  q 
                                  ∈ 
                                  B 
                                
                               (respectively,  
                               
                                  r 
                                  ∈ 
                                  F

Any transformation that moves  
                            θ 
                          outside the parameter space could be regarded as “invalid”. For instance, a transformation that maps a point  
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                          to another point  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  ′ 
                               
                            
                            ∉ 
                            Θ 
                          in which the set  
                            
                               { 
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     ′ 
                                  
                               
                               : 
                               K 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                               } 
                            
                          fails to be a probability distribution, is an invalid transformation.

Hence the question arises whether “valid” (i.e.,  inner ) transformations can be found everywhere in the parameter space, that is for every point  
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                         . If so then, by invoking  Theorems 1 and 4 , one can conclude that outcome preserving transformations can be found everywhere in the parameter space. In this section we show that, for the backward and forward transformations, the answer to this question is positive, provided that the transformations lie in some neighborhood of the identity.

In group theory, the set of all points to which a specific point  
                            θ 
                          can be moved by a transformation is known as the  orbit  of that point. For each point  
                            θ 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                         , each of the two groups  
                            
                               
                                  B 
                               
                               
                                  q 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  F 
                               
                               
                                  r 
                               
                            
                          gives rise to an orbit, which is a subset of  
                            
                               
                                  R 
                               
                               
                                  m 
                               
                            
                         . Here we are particularly interested in that part of the orbit that belongs to the parameter space  
                            Θ 
                         , that we name the  inner orbit  of the point.

The results in this section not only show that outcome preserving transformations can be found everywhere in the parameter space when the knowledge structure is graded in an item: They also provide insight about the local identifiability of the model.

We recall that a model is locally identifiable at a point  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          of the parameter space if its prediction function is one-to-one when restricted to some neighborhood of  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         . By  Corollary 1 , when the structure is either backward or forward graded, whatever small the neighborhood of  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                         , there always exists some outcome preserving transformation moving  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          to an equivalent point. As a consequence, whatever choice we make for such a neighborhood, the prediction function will never be one-to-one, entailing that the model is not even locally identifiable.

These results suggest that there should be a close connection between the transformational approach to identifiability followed here and the standard way of analyzing the local identifiability of a model (see, e.g.,  Goodman, 1974 ;  McHugh, 1956 ). This connection is considered in more detail in Section  6 .

So far, forward and backward gradedness, and the corresponding parameter transformations, have been examined with respect to single items, in isolation. Of course a knowledge structure can be graded in more than one item and when this happens we say that it is  graded in a set  of items. As an example, the knowledge structure on the set  
                         Q 
                         = 
                         
                            { 
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            , 
                            c 
                            , 
                            d 
                            , 
                            e 
                            } 
                         
                       given in  Doignon and Falmagne (1999)  and displayed in  Fig. 1 , happens to be graded in a set of items. In particular, this knowledge structure is graded in the subset  
                         
                            { 
                            a 
                            , 
                            b 
                            , 
                            d 
                            , 
                            e 
                            } 
                         
                      . In particular, it is forward graded in  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       and backward graded in  
                         d 
                       and  
                         e 
                      .

By the theoretical results obtained in previous sections, we know that there are inner transformations  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               d 
                            
                            
                               y 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , each of which moves the point  
                         θ 
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                      , while keeping constant the distribution on the response patterns. If the composition  
                         
                            
                               f 
                            
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                         ∘ 
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               d 
                            
                            
                               y 
                            
                         
                       of the two functions is formed, then a new parameter transformation is obtained that maps  
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               m 
                            
                         
                       into itself. It is thus clear that, by taking compositions of any number of such elementary backward and forward transformations, new and more sophisticated transformations can be obtained. To begin with, rather than being parameterized by a scalar  
                         x 
                         ∈ 
                         R 
                      , such transformation compositions are parameterized by  
                         k 
                      -dimensional vectors  
                         x 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            
                               R 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                      .

In this section we study the properties of transformation groups that emerge by forming compositions of backward and forward transformations. The results obtained in the previous sections are here extended to the general case of a knowledge structure graded in a set. Obviously the composition operation  
                         ∘ 
                       can be applied to transformations generated by different items in  
                         B 
                         ∪ 
                         F 
                      . The generalizations of the definitions of effective and inner transformations to the composition of transformations on different items are straightforward.

We shall start by recognizing that the composition of transformations generated by different items is indeed commutative. Given any two subsets  
                         X 
                         , 
                         Y 
                         ⊆ 
                         Q 
                      , let  
                         X 
                         ⊎ 
                         Y 
                       denote the disjoint union of the two sets.

It was shown in Section  4  that the backward and forward transformations generated by single items are a type of group actions, where the group is  
                            
                               ( 
                               R 
                               , 
                               + 
                               ) 
                            
                         . This result can now be generalized to transformations that are compositions of forward and/or backward transformations.

                               .

The backward and forward transformations  
                               
                                  b 
                                
                               and  
                               
                                  f 
                                
                               are group actions of the additive groups  
                               
                                  
                                     (

                                              |

                                              | 
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                     ,

                            B

                            F

The set of all transformations of the form  
                            
                               
                                  t 
                               
                               
                                  z 
                               
                            
                          is an abelian transformation group. This is the most general group that can be obtained by forming compositions of backward and forward transformations on different items.

Corollary 3

With the last theoretical result in this section we show that, like the unidimensional transformations, also compositions of forward and backward transformations are characterized by the property of being outcome preserving.

These results generalize to the  
                            k 
                         -dimensional case the conclusions concerning local identifiability, that have been drawn for the unidimensional case: if the knowledge structure is graded in a set, whatever small is the neighborhood of  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            ∈ 
                            Θ 
                          that one can consider, there always exist outcome preserving transformations moving  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                          to another point inside its neighborhood.

As already mentioned in previous sections, there is a close connection between the transformational approach proposed in the present article, and the standard way of analyzing the local identifiability of a probabilistic model. This last is based on the analysis of the Jacobian matrix of the prediction function  (Bamber & Van Santen, 2000; McHugh, 1956) . A model is locally identifiable at a point  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                       if the Jacobian matrix is full rank at that point (that is, the rank equals the number of dimensions of the domain). If not, then the null space of the Jacobian matrix plays a fundamental role in the determination of trade-offs among the unidentifiable parameters of the model.

This type of trade-off analysis was recently carried out with respect to the BLIM by  Heller (2017) , who provided an example in which the null space of the Jacobian was one-dimensional. The approach is briefly sketched here with a generalization to  
                         k 
                       dimensions. The Jacobian matrix of the prediction function of the BLIM is a  
                         
                            
                               2 
                            
                            
                               
                                  | 
                                  Q 
                                  | 
                               
                            
                         
                         − 
                         1 
                       times  
                         m 
                         − 
                         1 
                       matrix  
                         
                            
                               J 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        θ 
                                     
                                     
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       ∂ 
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             ϕ 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             R 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          ( 
                                                          θ 
                                                          ) 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ∂ 
                                                       
                                                          
                                                             θ 
                                                          
                                                          
                                                             i 
                                                          
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           θ 
                                           = 
                                           
                                              
                                                 θ 
                                              
                                              
                                                 0 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                      of the partial derivatives of the prediction function  
                         
                            
                               ϕ 
                            
                            
                               R 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            θ

                      , for each  
                         R

A main distinction between the transformational approach followed in this article and the Jacobian matrix approach concerns the domain and the codomain of the prediction function. In order to assure linear independence among the columns of the Jacobian matrix it is necessary to remove the column (dimension) corresponding to one of the knowledge state probabilities since, by the constrain  
                         
                            
                               ∑ 
                            
                            
                               K 
                               ∈ 
                               K 
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         1 
                      , it is trivially dependent on the remaining state probabilities. By convention, the column corresponding to the full set  
                         Q 
                       is removed. Similar considerations apply to the rows of the matrix. The constraint  
                         
                            
                               ∑ 
                            
                            
                               R 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     Q 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            
                               ϕ 
                            
                            
                               R 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            θ 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         1 
                       introduces a trivial dependence among the rows, which is removed by eliminating one of the rows of the matrix. By convention the removed row is, again, the one corresponding to the full set  
                         Q 
                      .

The task of determining parameter transformations via the null space of the Jacobian matrix is far from trivial. Heller obtained them, with the support of the Mathematica software, for a case in which the BLIM’s Jacobian had a one-dimensional null space. As a toy example, he considered the BLIM applied to the knowledge structure  
                         K 
                         = 
                         
                            { 
                            ∅ 
                            ,

                               , 
                               b 
                               }

                            }

                            b

                            c 
                            }

                         0 
                       for all  
                         q 
                         ∈ 
                         Q 
                      ). Although the example appeared to be very simple, as reported by Heller, computing the basis of the null space of the Jacobian matrix was an “extremely memory intensive task”.
