It was stated before that, in the multidimensional case, if  
                         
                            | 
                            B 
                            | 
                         
                         + 
                         
                            | 
                            F 
                            | 
                         
                       equals the number of dimensions of the null space of the Jacobian at  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               0 
                            
                         
                      , then the function  
                         
                            
                               t 
                            
                            
                               
                                  
                                     θ 
                                  
                                  
                                     0 
                                  
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         t 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  0 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       is a solution to the system of differential equations in  (10) . However, it should be observed that, for a  
                         k 
                      -dimensional null space, only  
                         
                            | 
                            B 
                            | 
                         
                         + 
                         
                            | 
                            F 
                            | 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         k 
                       can be concluded in general, and this may leave room for additional (though currently unknown, or not qualified) transformations, which are neither forward nor backward. Indeed, if this is the case, the mapping  
                         
                            
                               t 
                            
                            
                               θ 
                            
                         
                       examined here would only provide a partial view to the trade-off structure of an unidentifiable BLIM.

This section deals with two different ways of analyzing and visualizing trade-offs among the parameters of an unidentifiable BLIM: tradeoff graphs and tradeoff surfaces.

Going back again to the unidimensional (forward or backward) transformations, it was observed that, for  
                         x 
                         ≠ 
                         0 
                      , every unidimensional transformation is effective everywhere in  
                         Θ 
                      . On the other side, unidimensional transformations need not being effective at every single component  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                       of a vector  
                         θ 
                         ∈ 
                         Θ 
                      . When this happens, the component  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                       is said to be  invariant  with respect to the transformation itself. As an example, the component  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       is invariant with respect to the whole group  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       of backward transformations  
                         
                            
                               b 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               x 
                            
                         
                       whenever  
                         K 
                         ∉ 
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         ∪ 
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               − 
                            
                         
                      .

This notion of component-wise (or coordinate-wise) invariance gives rise to a binary relation  
                         V 
                       between the set  
                         D 
                         = 
                         
                            { 
                            1 
                            , 
                            2 
                            , 
                            … 
                            , 
                            k 
                            } 
                         
                       of the  
                         k 
                         = 
                         
                            | 
                            B 
                            | 
                         
                         + 
                         
                            | 
                            F 
                            | 
                         
                       dimensions of the group  
                         T 
                       on the one side, and the set  
                         P 
                       of all the parameters of the BLIM on the other side. Given any dimension  
                         j 
                         ∈ 
                         D 
                       and any parameter  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         P 
                      , the relation  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         V 
                         j 
                       holds true if  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                       is not invariant with respect to dimension  
                         j 
                      .

Remembering that composition of transformations commutes, since transformations in  
                         T 
                       are obtained as compositions of  
                         k 
                       distinct unidimensional backward and forward transformations taken in a certain order, there is a one-to-one correspondence between the dimensions in  
                         D 
                       and the set of unidimensional (backward and forward) transformation groups. Therefore, a component  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         P 
                       is invariant with respect to a dimension  
                         j 
                         ∈ 
                         D 
                       if and only if it is invariant with respect to the whole unidimensional transformation group corresponding to  
                         j 
                      . Hence,  Definitions 2 and 3  provide the necessary rules for constructing the relation  
                         V 
                      . In particular, if the unidimensional group corresponding to dimension  
                         j 
                       is  
                         
                            
                               B 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      , for some  
                         q 
                         ∈ 
                         B 
                      , then the only parameters that are in the relation  
                         V 
                       with  
                         j 
                       are  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         K 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         ∪ 
                         
                            
                               K 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               − 
                            
                         
                      . If the group corresponding to  
                         j 
                       is  
                         
                            
                               F 
                            
                            
                               r 
                            
                         
                      , for  
                         r 
                         ∈ 
                         F 
                      , then the only parameters that are in the relation  
                         V 
                       with  
                         j 
                       are  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               r 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       for all  
                         K 
                         ∈ 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̄ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                         ∪ 
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                                  
                                     ̄ 
                                  
                               
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                            
                               + 
                            
                         
                      .

The binary relation  
                         V 
                       can be graphically represented by means of a bipartite graph that we name  trade-off graph . If  
                         P 
                       represents the set of the BLIM’s  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               K 
                            
                         
                       parameters, and  
                         D 
                       the set of dimensions of  
                         T 
                      , the graph has as many vertices as  
                         
                            | 
                            P 
                            | 
                         
                         + 
                         
                            | 
                            D 
                            | 
                         
                      , and an undirected edge connects a parameter  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         P 
                       to a dimension  
                         j 
                         ∈ 
                         D 
                       whenever  
                         
                            
                               θ 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         V 
                         j 
                       holds true.

Example 4 
                         As an example, consider the knowledge space displayed in  Fig. 2 . It turns out to be forward-graded in both items  
                               1 
                             and  
                               2 
                            , so that  
                               F 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  } 
                               
                             and  
                               B 
                               = 
                               ∅ 
                            . 
                         Given this situation, the group  
                               T 
                             has two dimensions, and every transformation in  
                               T 
                             has the form  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               ∘ 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           x 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                             for  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               R 
                            . The binary relation  
                               V 
                             between the BLIM’s parameters and the two trade-off dimensions is displayed in  Table 3 . 
                         Parameters are in rows, whereas dimensions are in columns. Dimension 1 corresponds to the unidimensional forward transformation group  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            , whereas dimension 2 corresponds to  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            . Checkmarks indicate which parameters (rows) are not invariant with respect to each of the two dimensions (columns). 
                         The probability  
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                               
                             of each of the states in one of the two collections  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 K 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ̄ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        ∅ 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           2 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           2 
                                           , 
                                           3 
                                           , 
                                           4 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            and  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 K 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ̄ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                        
                                           + 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           1 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           1 
                                           , 
                                           2 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        Q 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            is in the relation  
                               V 
                             with dimension 1, whereas the probability  
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                               
                             of each of the states in one of the two collections  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 K 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ̄ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        ∅ 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           1 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           1 
                                           , 
                                           3 
                                           , 
                                           4 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            and  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 K 
                                              
                                              
                                                 ̄ 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                        
                                           + 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        { 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           2 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        
                                           { 
                                           1 
                                           , 
                                           2 
                                           } 
                                        
                                        , 
                                        Q 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                            is in the relation  
                               V 
                             with dimension 2. The trade-off graph obtained from the relation  
                               V 
                             is shown in  Fig. 3 , where vertices representing dimensions are displayed as empty circles, vertices representing probabilities  
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     K 
                                  
                               
                             as filled circles, and vertices representing  
                               
                                  
                                     η 
                                  
                                  
                                     q 
                                  
                               
                             parameters as filled boxes. 
                         Some observations concerning this graph are in order. In the first place the vertex of  
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        { 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        2 
                                        , 
                                        3 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is isolated from the rest of the graph, indicating that this parameter is invariant with respect to both dimensions and, hence, with respect to the whole group  
                               T 
                             of transformations. If the identifiability issues of the BLIM, for the knowledge structure at hand, were all confined to the forward gradedness in items  
                               1 
                             and  
                               2 
                            , then we would conclude that  
                               
                                  
                                     π 
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        { 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        2 
                                        , 
                                        3 
                                        } 
                                     
                                  
                               
                             is an identifiable parameter. 
                         In the second place, the probabilities of five knowledge states are connected to both dimensions, indicating that each of the two forward transformations  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             is effective at each of these five components of the parameter vector  
                               θ 
                            . This shows that some of the parameters may be simultaneously involved in more than a single trade-off dimension, creating a sort of link between dimensions.

As an example, consider the knowledge space displayed in  Fig. 2 . It turns out to be forward-graded in both items  
                               1 
                             and  
                               2 
                            , so that  
                               F 
                               = 
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  } 
                               
                             and  
                               B 
                               = 
                               ∅ 
                            .

Given this situation, the group  
                               T 
                             has two dimensions, and every transformation in  
                               T

                                     1

                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ∈ 
                               R 
                            . The binary relation  
                               V 
                             between the BLIM’s parameters and the two trade-off dimensions is displayed in  Table 3 .

Parameters are in rows, whereas dimensions are in columns. Dimension 1 corresponds to the unidimensional forward transformation group

                               T 
                             of transformations. If the identifiability issues of the BLIM, for the knowledge structure at hand, were all confined to the forward gradedness in items

In the second place, the probabilities of five knowledge states are connected to both dimensions, indicating that each of the two forward transformations  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             is effective at each of these five components of the parameter vector  
                               θ 
                            . This shows that some of the parameters may be simultaneously involved in more than a single trade-off dimension, creating a sort of link between dimensions.

Transformations in  
                         T 
                       have a geometrical representation in the two and three dimensions that can help in further clarifying the structure of the parameter trade-offs in an unidentifiable BLIM. For any given point

                               θ

                      .

The present paper was aimed at extending the theoretical results on the identifiability of structures FG or BG in one item, to the more general case of structures graded in a set of items. This task has been accomplished by means of an approach that looks at the identifiability of a structure from the viewpoint of the theory of groups. This formal tool allows for a combinatorial analysis of the specific knowledge structure at hand. In turn, this kind of analysis represents a diagnostic tool for detecting which parameters are not identifiable (i.e.  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               k 
                            
                         
                      ,  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      , and  
                         
                            
                               β 
                            
                            
                               q 
                            
                         
                      ), and what specific trade-offs among parameters do explain such unidentifiability. Interestingly, through this kind of approach it is possible to have information also about the identifiable parameters, that is, about the structural reasons that make them identifiable. All this information does not need knowledge about the exact values of such parameters, that is, the analysis is conducted exclusively on the combinatorial properties of the knowledge structure. Moreover, it has been shown how the approach based on parameter transformation analysis and the traditional method to study identifiability (i.e., by referring to the analysis of the Jacobian matrix) are two sides of the same coin. Furthermore, it has been shown how the proposed method can account for both local and global identifiability.

Identifiability is a well know concern in the area of latent class models. The parameter transformation analysis could represent a useful and interesting alternative tool for the study of identifiability of a model in general, and latent class models in particular. Among these, it has been recently shown by  Heller, Stefanutti, Anselmi, and Robusto (2015)  that the BLIM and its restrictions are formally equivalent to a number of probabilistic models, such as the Deterministic Input Noisy AND-gate model (DINA;  Haertel, 1984, 1989, 1990 ), the Deterministic Input OR-gate model (DINO;  Templin and Henson, 2006 ) and the Multiple Strategy DINA model  (DeLaTorre & Douglas, 2008) . Therefore, the theoretical results of this paper immediately transfer to these models, which are applied in the area of cognitive diagnostic modeling  (Tatsuoka, 1985) . In this connection, it is remarkable that identifiability in cognitive diagnostic models has been recently addressed by a number of authors (e.g.  Chen, Liu, Xu, & Ying, 2015 ;  DeCarlo, 2011 ;  Maris & Bechger, 2009 ;  Tatsuoka, 2009 ;  Xu & Zhang, 2016 ).

The theoretical results obtained in this paper may represent the starting point to extend the procedure described here to the investigation of the identifiability of any structure (i.e. not only for FG or BG structures) through an analytical approach based on the combinatorial properties of the structure at hand, and by means of different transformation types. For instance, if we look at the bottom-right panel of  Fig. 5  and suppose to keep constant the value on the vertical axis (i.e.,  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         
                      ), a projection (isometric curve) of the surface is obtained on the  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  η 
                               
                               
                                  1 
                               
                            
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  η 
                               
                               
                                  2 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         − 
                      plane. Such curve clearly shows that whenever  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         
                       is set to a constant value, the  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       parameters remain unidentifiable with a direct trade-off represented by the isometric curve. If this trade-off also exists for the particular choice  
                         
                            
                               π 
                            
                            
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  2 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                         
                         = 
                         0 
                       (which essentially amounts to removing the state  
                         
                            { 
                            1 
                            , 
                            2 
                            } 
                         
                       from the structure), this would have as a consequence that the two parameters  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               η 
                            
                            
                               2 
                            
                         
                       keep being unidentifiable in the knowledge structure  
                         K 
                         ∖ 
                         
                            { 
                            
                               { 
                               1 
                               , 
                               2 
                               } 
                            
                            } 
                         
                      , which is FG neither in item  
                         1 
                      , nor in item  
                         2 
                      . Hence, the isometric curve would represent another outcome preserving parameter transformation which is neither a forward- nor a backward-transformation. A thorough analysis of these different types of transformations is beyond this article.

The authors are grateful to Jean-Paul Doignon and two anonymous referees for their suggestions, that much contributed to improve the manuscript.

A group is a pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            G 
                            , 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       where  
                         G 
                       is a set and  
                         ⋅ 
                       is a binary operation mapping  
                         G 
                         × 
                         G 
                       to  
                         G 
                      , satisfying the following properties:  
                         a 
                         ⋅ 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                      ;  
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ⋅ 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         ⋅ 
                         c 
                         = 
                         a 
                         ⋅ 
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ⋅ 
                            c 
                            ) 
                         
                       for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         , 
                         c 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       (associativity), there is  
                         ϵ 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       such that  
                         a 
                         ⋅ 
                         ϵ 
                         = 
                         ϵ 
                         ⋅ 
                         a 
                         = 
                         a 
                       for all  
                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       (identity element); For every  
                         a 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       there is  
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                       such that  
                         a 
                         ⋅ 
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                         
                         = 
                         
                            
                               a 
                            
                            
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                         
                         ⋅ 
                         a 
                         = 
                         ϵ 
                       (inverse element). The group  
                         
                            ( 
                            G 
                            , 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is  abelian  (or  commutative ) if  
                         a 
                         ⋅ 
                         b 
                         = 
                         b 
                         ⋅ 
                         a 
                       for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                      .

Given a set  
                         V 
                      , a  group action  of  
                         
                            ( 
                            G 
                            , 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       on  
                         V 
                       is a mapping  
                         g 
                         : 
                         G 
                         × 
                         V 
                         → 
                         V 
                       satisfying the two axioms:

(1) 
                            Identity:  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     ϵ 
                                     , 
                                     v 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  v 
                                for all  
                                  v 
                                  ∈ 
                                  V 
                               
                            
                         
                         
                            (2) 
                            Compatibility:  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     g 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        v 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     ⋅ 
                                     b 
                                     , 
                                     v 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                for all  
                                  a 
                                  , 
                                  b 
                                  ∈ 
                                  G 
                                and all  
                                  v 
                                  ∈ 
                                  V 
                               .

Identity:  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     ϵ 
                                     , 
                                     v 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  = 
                                  v 
                                for all  
                                  v 
                                  ∈ 
                                  V

Compatibility:  
                                  g 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     a 
                                     , 
                                     g 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        b 
                                        , 
                                        v 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     )

                                  g 
                                  
                                     (

                                  a

                                  v

A group action is said  free  if, given  
                         g 
                         , 
                         h 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                      , the existence of a  
                         v 
                         ∈ 
                         V 
                       with  
                         g 
                         ⋅ 
                         v 
                         = 
                         h 
                         ⋅ 
                         v 
                       implies  
                         g 
                         = 
                         h 
                      . The function  
                         V 
                         → 
                         V 
                         : 
                         v 
                         ↦ 
                         g 
                         ⋅ 
                         v 
                       is termed a “transformation”. The  orbit  of an element  
                         v 
                         ∈ 
                         V 
                       is the set of elements in  
                         V 
                       to which  
                         v 
                       can be moved by the elements of  
                         G 
                      :  
                         
                            
                               G 
                               ⋅ 
                               v 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     g 
                                     ⋅ 
                                     v 
                                     ∣ 
                                     g 
                                     ∈ 
                                     G 
                                  
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                      The pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            G 
                            , 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a  topological group  if  
                         G 
                       is a topological space and, moreover, the binary operation  
                         ⋅ 
                       and the function that maps each group element to its inverse, are both continuous. In this respect we recall that the additive group  
                         
                            ( 
                            R 
                            , 
                            + 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a topological group, where the inverse of an element is obtained by taking its negative.

Finally, given two groups  
                         
                            ( 
                            G 
                            , 
                            ⋅ 
                            ) 
                         
                       and  
                         
                            ( 
                            H 
                            , 
                            ∗ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , a function  
                         h 
                         : 
                         G 
                         → 
                         H 
                       is a group homomorphism if it preserves the group operation (i.e.,  
                         h 
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ⋅ 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                         = 
                         h 
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∗ 
                         h 
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            ) 
                         
                       for all  
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         G 
                      ). If, moreover,  
                         h 
                       is a bijection, then it is an isomorphism.
